competitors.label: MDN script element
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference for script loading behavior, including defer, async, parser blocking, and execution timing.
theirStrengths: It is authoritative on script loading semantics and browser timing behavior.
theirGaps: It is broader than the interview prompt and less compact on the exact production bug teams hit when they confuse defer with async.

realWorldUseCases: Deferred scripts improve load performance by removing parser blocking while preserving execution order. The real pitfall is assuming defer behaves like async or running DOM-dependent code without understanding script timing.
actionableExamples: How Deferred Scripts Work Internally
followUpConfusion: Key Considerations - The defer attribute only works for **external scripts** (those with src attributes). - Inline scripts cannot use defer. - In old browsers (pre-HTML5), support for defer was inconsistent, but it’s now standard in all major browsers. - Combining defer and async on the same script is invalid — only one behavior applies.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains defer precisely, but our page makes the real production decision clearer: parser blocking, DOM readiness, and why defer is not just async with a different name.
actionableExamples: The canonical docs still have strong timing examples, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more direct on the next confusion interviewers ask about: external-only support, order guarantees, and defer versus async semantics.

actionableExamples: Add one tiny normal-script versus defer versus async timing snippet later if we need more example distance over MDN.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical script-loading reference, but our page is stronger for this prompt because it keeps the answer centered on parser blocking, execution order, and DOM-timing bugs.

theirGaps: It is less explicit on the DOM-dependent timing pitfalls and on defer's external-script-only restriction.

realWorldUseCases: Deferred scripts improve load performance by removing parser blocking while preserving execution order. The real pitfall is assuming defer behaves like async or running DOM-dependent code without understanding script timing.
actionableExamples: How Deferred Scripts Work Internally
followUpConfusion: Key Considerations - The defer attribute only works for **external scripts** (those with src attributes). - Inline scripts cannot use defer. - In old browsers (pre-HTML5), support for defer was inconsistent, but it’s now standard in all major browsers. - Combining defer and async on the same script is invalid — only one behavior applies.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd is strong interview prep, but our page is more explicit about the production failure mode: DOM-dependent code running under the wrong mental model.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is direct interview prep, but our page is stronger because it ties defer to parser behavior, execution order, and the exact DOM-timing bugs teams ship.

theirGaps: It is less compressed as an interview answer and lighter on the production framing around DOM-dependent code.

realWorldUseCases: Deferred scripts improve load performance by removing parser blocking while preserving execution order. The real pitfall is assuming defer behaves like async or running DOM-dependent code without understanding script timing.

realWorldUseCases: javascript.info is strong on the timing model, but our page is more explicit about when defer is the right production default for DOM-dependent application scripts.
